raw_case:
Gastric hamartomatous inverted polyps have high internal echoes: A case report
A 58-year-old woman with an elevated lesion in the stomach was referred to our hospital. Gastroscopy showed a lobulated mass resembling a submucosal tumor. The tumor showed early enhancement on computed tomography. Endoscopic ultrasound showed a lobulated tumor with distinct borders, no attenuated posterior echoes, and high internal echoes. Fine needle aspiration biopsy pathologically showed normal gastric mucosa and spindle cells growing sparsely with SMA positivity and DOG1/S100 negativities, suggesting an inflammatory fibroid polyp. The patient, therefore, underwent laparoscopy endoscopy cooperative surgery to the gastric lesion. Postoperative pathological study showed downward mucosal proliferation into the submucosal layer in a lobulated manner with hyperplasia of the crypt epithelium. Some part of the lesion was located under the normal orthotopic gastric mucosa. These pathologic findings led us to the diagnosis of gastric hamartomatous inverted polyp (GHIP). In conclusion, endoscopy specialists should note that fibrous component-rich tumors have low internal echoes and attenuated posterior echoes, whereas GHIPs have high internal echoes and no attenuated posterior echoes.

Introduction

Similar to computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), ultrasound is an extremely useful diagnostic modality in various solid malignancies. Ultrasound, however, has limited efficacy against hollow organ diseases such as gastric cancers and colon cancers due to the highly probable presence of air just around the lesions.Endoscopic ultrasonography (EUS) can depict submucosal tumors through ultrasound image acquisition by directly contacting the ultrasound probe to the mucosa just above the lesion. In addition, fine needle aspiration (FNA) biopsy under EUS guidance plays important roles in the diagnosis of submucosal tumors.Gastric hamartomatous inverted polyps (GHIPs) are rare disorders of the stomach and are characterized by their downward growth of hyperplastic mucosal component into the submucosal layer [[1], [2], [3]]. GHIPs develop predominantly in the upper part of the stomach, but EUS findings of them remain uncertain.We experienced this rare GHIP case and herein report that this disorder should have high internal echoes due to its pathological structures.

Case presentation

A 58-year-old woman had been diagnosed with a protruding lesion in her stomach on a medical health check. Endoscopy for detailed examination clarified the chronic gastritis and a gastric tumor 3 years before. Positive urine Helicobacter pylori (H. pylori) antibody showed the chronic gastritis to be caused by H. Pylori infection followed by successful antibiotic therapy. Under the tentative diagnosis of possible gastric cancer, endoscopic biopsy was done to the gastric tumor and showed the tumor not to be gastric cancer, but to be hyperplastic polyp. The patient, therefore, underwent annual endoscopic checkups for the atrophic gastritis. Two years later, follow-up CT showed that the elevated lesion was 26mm in size, located close to the esophago-gastric junction, had a similar Hounsfield Unit value to that of the gastric wall, and had strong early enhancement (Fig. 1). Repeated gastroscopy showed a lobulated mass resembling a submucosal tumor (Fig. 2) that had distinct borders, no attenuated posterior echoes, and high internal echoes on EUS (Fig. 3). FNA biopsy pathologically showed normal gastric mucosa and spindle cells growing sparsely with SMA positivity and DOG1 / S100 negativities, suggesting an inflammatory fibroid polyp [4,5]. The patient, therefore, underwent laparoscopy endoscopy cooperative surgery (LECS) to the gastric lesion both for definitive diagnosis and treatment [[6], [7], [8]]. LECS consisted of endoscopic submucosal dissection of the lesion followed by laparoscopic inversion suture of the dissected thin gastric wall. Postoperative pathological study showed downward mucosal proliferation in a lobulated manner with hyperplasia of the crypt epithelium, sparse fibroblast-like spindle cells growing in a storiform pattern, and no eosinophil infiltration. Some part of the lesion was located under the normal orthotopic gastric mucosa. These pathologic findings led us to the diagnosis of GHIP (Fig. 4). The patient recovered uneventfully, was discharged on the postoperative 7th day, and was scheduled to receive annual endoscopic follow-ups.

Discussion

The World Health Organization classifies nonepithelial digestive tumors by their pathological origins, i.e., Cajal cells, adipose tissue, (myosin) fibroblast, smooth muscle, skeletal muscle, vessel, nerve, and uncertain [9]. Each origin shows a positive result for specific immune-markers. We, therefore, can easily rule out the possibility of neurogenic tumors and gastro-intestinal stromal tumors due to the SMA positivity and S100/DOG1 negativities in this case. In addition, CD34 positivity further led us to rule out the leiomyoma.Inflammatory fibroid polyp is a fibroblast-derived disease with few cellular components and eosinophil infiltration [4,5]. It is a rare disorder often found in the stomach and ileum with possible CD34 and SMA positivities, having led us to the initial tentative diagnosis of inflammatory fibroid polyp in this case. This disorder, however, should present low internal echoes due to its fibrous component dominant characteristics. Low internal echoes should also be observed in fibrous component-rich various diseases such as desmoid fibromatosis, leiomyoma, and shwannoma. In addition, massive fibrous components generally cause delayed mass enhancement on images, markedly conflicting to the image findings in this case.Hamartomas are intermediate disorders between tumors and malformations, in which certain tissue components are present in ratios different from those normally possessed by each organ. Hamartomas, therefore, are well-defined masses with an abundant fat component in the breast hamartomas and with a large quantity of cartilage component in the lung hamartomas. GHIP is neither a pure hamartoma nor a submucosal tumor. However, the pathological components of GHIP are essentially the same as those of hamartomas: marked mucosal proliferation and small amount of fibroblast-like spindle cell proliferation in this case. When the proliferated mucosal clusters are found submucosally, the presence of papillary structures and micro voids in the GFIP causes large ultrasound wave backscattering, making the internal echoes of the tumor high. Even if fibrous components are present in GHIP, they are usually small in amount, generally leading to no posterior echo attenuation as was seen in this case.Nonepithelial tumors generally possess varying degree of fibrous components, often causing immunostaining use for the definitive diagnosis in many cases. Immunostaining results suggested the diagnosis of inflammatory fibroid polyp in this case. Diagnostic physicians, however, can correctly diagnose GHIPs with pathological component-based image evaluation.

Conclusion

GHIPs are rare gastric disorders and have proliferated gastric mucosa in the tumors.The presence of gastric mucosa with papillary structures and/or micro voids generates large ultrasound wave backscattering, making the internal echoes of the tumor high. Diagnostic physicians should note that GHIPs have high internal echoes and no attenuated posterior echoes.

Patient consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for the publication of this case report and any accompanying images.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 58-year-old woman
- Chief Complaint: Elevated lesion in the stomach 
- History of Present Illness: Protruding gastric lesion identified during a routine health check three years prior; previously identified during endoscopy as a hyperplastic polyp with chronic Helicobacter pylori (H. pylori)–induced gastritis treated successfully with antibiotics; no significant symptoms were reported.
- Past Medical History: Treated H. pylori infection; chronic gastritis managed with annual endoscopic follow-ups.
- Personal History: None.
- Family History: None.
- Physical Examination: Not explicitly described.
- Ancillary Tests: 
  - Gastroscopy: Repeated examinations showed a lobulated mass resembling a submucosal tumor with high internal echoes and no attenuated posterior echoes on endoscopic ultrasound (EUS).
  - Fine-Needle Aspiration (FNA) Biopsy: Normal gastric mucosa and spindle cells with SMA positivity and DOG1/S100 negativities suggested an inflammatory fibroid polyp.
  - Computed Tomography (CT): Demonstrated a 26-mm elevated lesion near the esophago-gastric junction with early enhancement and similar Hounsfield Units to the gastric wall.
  - Histopathological (Postoperative): Downward mucosal proliferation into the submucosal layer with hyperplastic crypt epithelium. Sparse spindle cells with no eosinophil infiltration were observed. Final pathology revealed a gastric hamartomatous inverted polyp (GHIP).
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Differential Diagnosis:**
   - The patient's gastric lesion and imaging findings initially raised concerns for gastric cancer or inflammatory fibroid polyp due to its prominent features.
   - Biopsy findings ruled out gastric cancer and suggested an inflammatory fibroid polyp based on spindle cell growth and SMA-positive/DOG1- and S100-negative markers.

2. **Refining the Diagnosis:**
   - The patient underwent laparoscopy endoscopy cooperative surgery (LECS) for definitive diagnosis and treatment. Postoperative histology revealed features inconsistent with an inflammatory fibroid polyp (e.g., absence of significant fibrous components or eosinophil infiltration).

3. **Ruling Out Other Submucosal Tumors:**
   - Differential markers and imaging findings ruled out leiomyoma, schwannoma, and other fibrous tumors due to the tumor's unique high internal echoes, absence of posterior attenuation, and microscopically observed proliferation of gastric mucosa.

4. **Final Diagnosis of GHIP:**
   - The distinct pathological findings of downward mucosal growth, crypt epithelial hyperplasia, and occasional spindle cells confirmed the rare diagnosis of gastric hamartomatous inverted polyp. The EUS finding of high internal echoes with no posterior echo attenuation further supported this conclusion.

results.content:
<think>
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient's history. She's a 58-year-old woman with a known hyperplastic polyp found three years ago during a routine check. That was treated for H. pylori, which was successful. Now, she has a new lesion in the stomach. The key points here are the progression from a hyperplastic polyp to a more complex lesion.

The current gastroscopy shows a lobulated mass that looks like a submucosal tumor with high internal echoes on EUS. The FNA biopsy found spindle cells positive for SMA but negative for DOG1 and S100, which points towards an inflammatory fibroid polyp. But the postoperative histopathology says it's a gastric hamartomatous inverted polyp (GHIP). Wait, how does that fit?

GHIP is a rare type of polyp that's usually benign. The features mentioned—mucosal proliferation into the submucosa with hyperplastic crypts and sparse spindle cells without eosinophils—align with GHIP. The EUS findings of a submucosal tumor with high echoes might be confusing because GHIPs are typically mucosal, but maybe the lesion is larger and has some submucosal components. The CT scan shows early enhancement similar to the gastric wall, which could be consistent with a benign lesion.

The initial FNA suggested an inflammatory fibroid polyp, but the final pathology is GHIP. Inflammatory fibroid polyps are usually benign too, but they have more spindle cells and sometimes eosinophils. Since the post-op pathology is definitive, GHIP is the correct diagnosis here. The patient's history of H. pylori and chronic gastritis might contribute to the development of such a polyp. So the answer should be GHIP.
</think>

```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient's history reveals a previously treated H. pylori infection and chronic gastritis, with a prior hyperplastic polyp identified three years ago. The current lesion's evolution from a hyperplastic polyp to a more complex mass necessitates differentiation between benign and malignant entities.  
<step 2> Gastroscopy and EUS findings describe a lobulated submucosal tumor with high internal echoes, which is atypical for a standard hyperplastic polyp but aligns with submucosal tumor characteristics. However, the absence of posterior echo attenuation suggests a non-solid, possibly mucosal-based lesion.  
<step 3> The FNA biopsy initially suggested an inflammatory fibroid polyp (SMA+ and DOG1/S100−), a benign lesion, but the histopathological findings of downward mucosal proliferation, hyperplastic crypt epithelium, and sparse spindle cells without eosinophils are more consistent with a gastric hamartomatous inverted polyp (GHIP), a rare benign variant.  
<step 4> CT findings of early enhancement and Hounsfield Units similar to gastric wall further support a benign, non-malignant process. The absence of malignant markers (e.g., dysplasia, infiltrative growth) in the final pathology confirms GHIP as the diagnosis.  

### Answer:
Gastric hamartomatous inverted polyp (GHIP)
```
